title: How to Find a Hidden PDF of Any Academic Article
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You find the perfect academic article for your research paper. Then you see the price: $40 to download. For one article. You can't afford it. Your library might not have access. Don't pay. Here is how to find academic papers for free using legal methods.

Google Scholar (Your First Stop)

Go to scholar.google.com. Search for your article. Look to the right of the search result. You'll often see a PDF link. Click it. If the author uploaded a preprint, you can read it for free.

Not all articles have PDF links. But many do, especially for older articles and papers in the sciences. Always check Google Scholar first.

The DOI Trick

Every academic article has a Digital Object Identifier (DOI). It looks like a code: 10.xxxx/xxxx. Copy the DOI. Paste it into Google. Often, the first result is a free PDF hosted on an institutional repository or author's personal website.

Search for: 'doi:10.1016/j.tate.2013.07.002 pdf' (replace with your DOI). This sometimes bypasses publisher paywalls.

Unpaywall (Browser Extension)

Install the Unpaywall browser extension (free, available for Chrome and Firefox). When you land on a paywalled article page, Unpaywall searches for a legal free version. If it finds one, a green tab appears on the side of your screen. Click it. Read the PDF.

Unpaywall finds free versions about 50% of the time. It's legal because it links to author-uploaded preprints and institutional repository copies.

PubMed Central (For Science and Medical Papers)

If your article is in the life sciences or medicine, search PubMed Central (pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov). Many federally funded studies are required to be posted here for free within 12 months of publication.

Even if the publisher charges, the free version exists on PMC. Use it.

Your University Library (The Most Reliable)

Your library subscribes to databases. You can access them for free as a student. Go to your library's website. Search for the journal. Log in with your student ID. Download the PDF.

If your library doesn't have access, use interlibrary loan. They'll get the article from another library for free. It takes a few days. Plan ahead.

Email the Author (Works Surprisingly Often)

Find the author's email address on their university website or ResearchGate profile. Send a polite email:

'Dear Professor [Name], I'm a student at [University]. I'm writing a paper on [topic]. I saw your article '[Title]' and would love to read it. Would you be willing to send me a PDF? Thank you for your time.'

Most academics are happy to share their work. They don't get paid for journal subscriptions. They'd rather you read their research than not.

What Not to Do

Don't use Sci-Hub. It's a pirate site. It provides free access to nearly every academic article. It's also illegal. Many universities block it. Using it could get you in trouble. Stick to legal methods.

You can find almost any article legally if you try these methods. Google Scholar first. Unpaywall second. PubMed Central for science. Your library third. Email the author last. Don't pay $40. Your research budget shouldn't be spent on single articles.
